questions.question: What makes a mistake log more useful than simply remembering a bad mock?
options: It turns repeated failures into searchable patterns with explicit corrective actions.
It guarantees that the same prompt will never be reused.
It allows the learner to avoid taking notes elsewhere.
It replaces the need for peer feedback.
questions.explanation: The real value is pattern detection and follow-through.

questions.question: Which field is most important for converting a logged mistake into progress?
options: A concrete corrective drill or next action.
The exact color of the original diagram.
A numerical ranking of embarrassment level.
A full transcript of the interview.
questions.explanation: Without a next action, the log records pain but not improvement.

questions.question: Why should mistake categories stay controlled instead of growing freely?
options: Stable categories make trend analysis and retesting far easier over time.
Because distributed systems mistakes always fit exactly three labels.
Because mocks are invalid unless categories never change.
Because only interviewer-led sessions may create new labels.
questions.explanation: A controlled vocabulary helps surface recurring patterns.

questions.question: Which failure mode makes a mistake log emotionally loud but operationally weak?
options: Every entry includes a corrective action.
Entries describe frustration but omit root cause and retest plans.
Categories are reused across prompts.
The log links mistakes back to lessons and mocks.
questions.explanation: Ventilation is understandable, but it does not create a learning loop by itself.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with keeping old resolved mistakes instead of deleting them?
options: It preserves trend visibility and confidence evidence, but it can add noise unless statuses are maintained.
It prevents any new categories from being added.
It makes it impossible to focus on current weaknesses.
It raises storage costs enough to require archival tiers.
questions.explanation: Historical visibility is useful, but only if entries remain easy to filter.

questions.question: Which signal best shows the feedback loop is actually closing?
options: The number of entries logged each day.
How often mistakes are retested and either resolved or shown to recur in later sessions.
The total amount of markdown written in the log.
How many prompts were borrowed from public repositories.
questions.explanation: Improvement comes from re-encountering the weakness with evidence.

questions.question: If the same mistake appears across unrelated prompts, what does that usually imply?
options: The prompt bank is low quality.
The issue is likely a process or communication habit rather than a one-off technical miss.
The learner should stop doing mocks for a week.
The category system is too detailed to be useful.
questions.explanation: Cross-prompt recurrence often reveals a deeper habit worth targeted drilling.

questions.question: How should the system treat a mistake fixed in solo practice but still present in live mocks?
options: Mark it fully resolved because the technical concept was understood once.
Track it as partially improved and note that pressure, pacing, or communication still trigger it.
Delete the old entries and start a new category.
Ignore it because mocks add too much noise.
questions.explanation: Pressure-sensitive failures still matter in interview prep even when the concept seems understood.
